info.name: Feature Flag & A/B Testing Service
info.description:
Complete API test collection for the Feature Flag & A/B Testing Service built with Spring Boot.

## How to use
1. Import this collection into Postman.
2. Make sure the application is running on localhost:8080 (`make run`).
3. Execute requests in the order they appear — each folder builds on the previous one.

## Collection variables
- `baseUrl` — application base URL
- `adminKey` — API key for admin endpoints
- `clientKey` — API key for evaluation endpoints
- `flagKey` — reused across requests (auto-set by the Create Flag request)
- `abFlagKey` — key for the A/B test flag
- `targetedFlagKey` — key for the targeted flag

item.name: 01 — Health Check

request.description: Returns all Prometheus metrics in text format. Look for feature_flag_* custom metrics after the service has processed some requests.
Opens the Swagger UI. Best viewed in a browser. Navigate to http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html directly.

Confirms that unauthenticated requests are rejected. No X-API-Key header is sent.

Expected: 401 Unauthorized with {"error": "Missing X-API-Key header"}

item.name: 05. Auth check — client key on admin endpoint returns 403

request.description:
Confirms that a client key cannot access admin endpoints.

Expected: 403 Forbidden

item.description: Verify the service and all its dependencies are up before running any other requests. Run this first every time.

item.name: 02 — Boolean Flag (Percentage Rollout)

request.description:
Creates a boolean flag that is enabled but has 0% rollout. This is the standard pattern for deploying a feature that is not yet visible to any user.

Expected: 201 Created with the flag object including a generated UUID and timestamps.
Returns all flags in the system. Should contain at least the flag created in the previous request.

Retrieves the flag created in request 01 by its key.

Expected: 200 OK with the full flag object.

exec: pm.test('Flag is disabled for all users at 0%', () => {

request.description:
Evaluates the flag for a user when rollout is 0%. No user should receive this flag.

Expected: { "enabled": false, "variant": null, "reason": "DEFAULT" }

Updates the flag rollout to 50%. After this, approximately half of all users should receive the flag as enabled.

This also triggers a Redis cache update and an SSE broadcast to all connected clients.

Evaluates for user-abc-123 at 50% rollout. Result is deterministic — this user always gets the same outcome.

Expected: enabled is true or false, reason is ROLLOUT or DEFAULT.
The X-Trace-Id header in the response confirms trace ID propagation is working.
Evaluates for a different user at 50% rollout. Different users hash to different buckets, so this user may get a different result to user-abc-123 — demonstrating the consistent hashing at work.

item.name: 08. Evaluate consistency check — same user, same result

request.description: Re-evaluates user-abc-123. The result must be identical to request 06 — same user always gets the same outcome due to consistent hashing.
Sets enabled to false. This is the killswitch — the flag immediately returns false for every user regardless of rollout percentage or targeting rules.

exec: pm.test('Flag is DISABLED for all users', () => {

request.description:
After the killswitch is activated, every user must receive enabled=false with reason=DISABLED — even users who were previously in the rollout.

Expected: { "enabled": false, "variant": null, "reason": "DISABLED" }
Re-enables the flag and sets rollout to 100% — all users now receive the flag.

item.name: 12. Evaluate at 100% — expect ROLLOUT for all users

exec: pm.test('Flag is enabled for all users at 100%', () => {

request.description:
At 100% rollout every user must receive enabled=true with reason=ROLLOUT.

Expected: { "enabled": true, "variant": null, "reason": "ROLLOUT" }

Confirms that evaluating a flag that doesn't exist returns 404 rather than silently returning false.

Expected: 404 Not Found

item.description:
Tests the core boolean flag lifecycle:
create → verify → evaluate → update rollout → disable (killswitch) → re-enable → delete.

Run these requests in order from top to bottom.

item.name: 03 — A/B Testing

request.description:
Creates an A/B test flag with two variants at equal weight. rolloutPercentage is 100 so every user is included in the experiment.

Expected: 201 Created with variants array in the response.

exec: // Save alice's variant for the consistency check

request.description:
Evaluates the A/B flag for user-alice. The variant field in the response will be either 'control' or 'treatment'.

Expected: enabled=true, variant is 'control' or 'treatment', reason='ROLLOUT'
Evaluates for user-bob. Compare this result to user-alice's — they may receive different variants, demonstrating the consistent hashing distribution.

exec: pm.test('Variant is identical to first evaluation', () => {

request.description: Re-evaluates user-alice. The variant must be identical to what she received in request 02, proving consistent hashing is working correctly across multiple calls.

Creates a 3-variant flag. Weights must sum to exactly 100. Demonstrates that the service supports multi-variant experiments beyond simple A/B splits.

Expected: 201 Created

exec: pm.test('Variant is one of the three options', () => {

request.description: Evaluates the 3-variant flag. Variant will be one of: control, treatment-a, or treatment-b.

item.description:
Tests A/B flag creation and variant assignment.

Verifies that:
- Variants are assigned based on consistent hashing
- The same user always gets the same variant
- Different users can get different variants
- Variant weights control distribution

item.name: 04 — Targeting Rules

request.description:
Creates a flag with rolloutPercentage=0 and a targeting rule.
Only users whose 'plan' attribute equals 'enterprise' will receive this flag. All others receive DEFAULT.

Expected: 201 Created

Evaluates for a user with plan=enterprise. The targeting rule matches, so the user receives the flag even though rollout is 0%.

Expected: { "enabled": true, "reason": "TARGETED" }

Evaluates for a user with plan=free. The targeting rule does not match, rollout is 0%, so the user receives DEFAULT.

Expected: { "enabled": false, "reason": "DEFAULT" }

Creates a flag using the IN operator. Any user whose plan is one of pro, team, or enterprise will match.

Expected: 201 Created

item.name: 05. Evaluate IN operator — pro user (expect TARGETED)

exec: pm.test('Pro user is TARGETED via IN operator', () => {

request.description:
Pro plan user matches the IN operator targeting rule.

Expected: { "enabled": true, "reason": "TARGETED" }

Creates a flag using the GREATER_THAN operator on a numeric attribute. Users with age > 17 will match.

Expected: 201 Created

User with age 25 is greater than 17 — matches the rule.

Expected: { "enabled": true, "reason": "TARGETED" }

User with age 15 is not greater than 17 — does not match.

Expected: { "enabled": false, "reason": "DEFAULT" }

item.description:
Tests attribute-based targeting rules.

Verifies all six operators: EQUALS, NOT_EQUALS, CONTAINS, IN, GREATER_THAN, LESS_THAN.

A targeted user receives enabled=true with reason=TARGETED regardless of rollout percentage.

item.name: 05 — Batch Evaluation

request.description:
Evaluates three flags in a single request for the same user. Returns an array of results in the same order as the input.

This is the recommended pattern for application startup — fetch all relevant flags in one round trip rather than three separate calls.

Expected: 200 OK with an array of three evaluation responses.

item.name: 02. Batch evaluate — mixed valid and invalid flags

request.description: Sends a batch request where one flag exists and one does not. Observe how the service handles the mix — the second request will return 404.

item.description: Tests the batch evaluation endpoint which allows evaluating multiple flags in a single HTTP request.

item.name: 06 — Real-Time SSE Updates
01. Open SSE stream (keep this tab open)

request.description:
Opens a persistent SSE connection. The response panel stays open and displays events as they arrive.

Keep this tab open and send flag update requests from other tabs to see events appear here in real time.

Each event looks like:  
event: flag-change  
data: {"key":"...","enabled":true,...}

item.name: 02. Trigger SSE event — update boolean flag

request.description:
Send this request while the SSE stream (request 01) is open in another tab. You should immediately see a flag-change event appear in the SSE tab with the updated flag data.

This demonstrates the full pub/sub pipeline:
Admin API → PostgreSQL → Redis cache → Redis pub/sub → FlagChangeListener → SseFlagBroadcaster → SSE client
Sends another flag change with enabled=false. Watch for a second event in the SSE stream tab showing the flag is now disabled.
Re-enables the flag at 100% rollout. A third SSE event should appear in the stream tab.

item.description:
Tests the Server-Sent Events streaming endpoint.

HOW TO TEST THIS FOLDER:
1. Send request 01 (Open SSE stream) and leave it open — do not close the tab.
2. Open a new Postman tab.
3. Send any flag update request (e.g. request 02 or 03).
4. Switch back to the SSE tab and watch the event appear in real time.

The event should appear within milliseconds of the flag update.

item.name: 07 — Analytics & Impressions

request.description:
Evaluates the flag to generate an impression record. Send this request 4-5 times using Postman's 'Send' button to generate multiple impressions. Each evaluation is recorded asynchronously in the impressions table.

Tip: Use Postman's Collection Runner or simply click Send multiple times.
Evaluates the A/B flag to generate impressions with variant data. Send multiple times to build up impression history for the stats endpoint.

item.name: 04. Get flag stats — A/B flag with variant breakdown
05. Get stats for non-existent flag — expect 404

request.description:
Confirms that requesting stats for a flag that doesn't exist returns 404.

Expected: 404 Not Found

item.description:
Tests the impression logging and analytics endpoint.

Every call to /evaluate records an impression asynchronously. After making several evaluation requests, the stats endpoint shows the accumulated count.

item.name: 08 — Validation & Error Handling
01. Create flag with invalid key — expect 400

request.description:
Flag keys must be lowercase alphanumeric with hyphens. Uppercase and underscores are rejected by the @Pattern validation.

Expected: 400 Bad Request

Rollout percentage must be between 0 and 100. Values outside this range are rejected by @Min and @Max validation.

Expected: 400 Bad Request

item.name: 03. Create flag with missing name — expect 400

request.description:
The name field is required (@NotBlank). Omitting it should return 400.

Expected: 400 Bad Request

Attempts to create a flag with the same key as the one created in folder 02. Flag keys must be unique.

Expected: 409 Conflict

userId is required for evaluation (@NotBlank). Omitting it should return 400.

Expected: 400 Bad Request

item.description:
Tests input validation and error responses.

Verifies that the API rejects malformed requests with descriptive error messages.

item.name: 09 — Cleanup

request.description:
Deletes the boolean flag created in folder 02.

Expected: 204 No Content

Deletes the A/B test flag.

Expected: 204 No Content

Deletes the enterprise targeting flag.

Expected: 204 No Content

Deletes the 3-variant pricing page flag.

Expected: 204 No Content

Deletes the IN operator flag.

Expected: 204 No Content

Deletes the GREATER_THAN operator flag.

Expected: 204 No Content
Lists all flags to confirm the database is clean. Should return an empty array if all flags were deleted successfully.

item.description:
Deletes all flags created during testing.

Run this folder at the end of a test session to leave the database clean for the next run.
